

Here, we focus on linear solid mechanics, which involves analyzing the behavior of elastic bodies through a system of partial differential equations (PDEs) that govern their deformation. The solver at hand is specifically designed for 3D/2D unstructured mesh problems and operates as a linear elasticity solver utilizing the finite element method (FEM).

The primary objective of the solver is to determine the vector solution for the unknown displacements within the elastic body. By utilizing FEM, it efficiently searches for a solution that satisfies the given boundary conditions and accurately captures the deformation characteristics of the system. This solver serves as a valuable tool for investigating and understanding the mechanical behavior of elastic materials in various engineering applications.\hypertarget{md__tmp_opencode_sharc_src_CAWF_Elasticity_Readme_autotoc_md22}{}\doxysection{Mathematics}\label{md__tmp_opencode_sharc_src_CAWF_Elasticity_Readme_autotoc_md22}
\hypertarget{md__tmp_opencode_sharc_src_CAWF_Elasticity_Readme_autotoc_md23}{}\doxysubsubsection{Problem description}\label{md__tmp_opencode_sharc_src_CAWF_Elasticity_Readme_autotoc_md23}
Under small elastic deformation, the steady elastic deformation equation (linear elastic system) on domain \$\textbackslash{}\+Omega\$ reads

\$\$ -\/\textbackslash{}nabla\textbackslash{}cdot\textbackslash{}sigma(\textbackslash{}mathbf\{x\})=\textbackslash{}mathbf\{f\}(\textbackslash{}mathbf\{x\}) \textbackslash{}quad \textbackslash{}mathbf\{x\}\textbackslash{}in\textbackslash{}\+Omega \$\$

here, \$\textbackslash{}sigma(\textbackslash{}mathbf\{x\})\$ is stress tensor, \$\textbackslash{}mathbf\{f\}(\textbackslash{}mathbf\{x\})\$ is the body force per unit volume. The stress tensor \$\textbackslash{}sigma(\textbackslash{}mathbf\{x\})\$ under isotropic elastic conditions is given by

\$\$ \textbackslash{}sigma(\textbackslash{}mathbf\{x\}) = \textbackslash{}lambda(\textbackslash{}nabla\textbackslash{}cdot\textbackslash{}mathbf\{u\}(\textbackslash{}mathbf\{x\}))\textbackslash{}mathbb\{I\} + \textbackslash{}mu (\textbackslash{}nabla\textbackslash{}mathbf\{u\}(\textbackslash{}mathbf\{x\}) + \textbackslash{}left(\textbackslash{}nabla\textbackslash{}mathbf\{u\}(\textbackslash{}mathbf\{x\})\textbackslash{}right)$^\wedge$\textbackslash{}text\{T\}) \$\$

here, \$\textbackslash{}lambda\textbackslash{}in\textbackslash{}mathbb\{R\}$^\wedge$\{+\}\$ and \$\textbackslash{}mu\textbackslash{}in\textbackslash{}mathbb\{R\}$^\wedge$\{+\}\$ are the Lame\textquotesingle{}s elasticity parameters for the homogeneous material, \$\textbackslash{}mathbb\{I\}\$ is the identity tensor, and \$\textbackslash{}mathbf\{u\}(\textbackslash{}mathbf\{x\})\$ is the displacement field vector. This governing PDE iis also knows as Navier\textquotesingle{}s equation.\hypertarget{md__tmp_opencode_sharc_src_CAWF_Elasticity_Readme_autotoc_md24}{}\doxysubsubsection{Variational formulation}\label{md__tmp_opencode_sharc_src_CAWF_Elasticity_Readme_autotoc_md24}
Without entering into the details, the variational formulation for the Navier\textquotesingle{}s equation reads

\$\$ \textbackslash{}int\+\_\+\{\textbackslash{}\+Omega\} \textbackslash{}lambda \textbackslash{}nabla \textbackslash{}cdot \textbackslash{}mathbf\{u\}(\textbackslash{}mathbf\{x\}) \textbackslash{}nabla \textbackslash{}cdot \textbackslash{}mathbf\{v\}(\textbackslash{}mathbf\{x\}) + 2\textbackslash{}mu\textbackslash{}varepsilon(\textbackslash{}mathbf\{u\}(\textbackslash{}mathbf\{x\}))\+:\textbackslash{}varepsilon(\textbackslash{}mathbf\{u\}(\textbackslash{}mathbf\{x\})) -\/ \textbackslash{}int\+\_\+\{\textbackslash{}\+Omega\}\textbackslash{}mathbf\{f\}(\textbackslash{}mathbf\{x\})\textbackslash{}cdot\{\textbackslash{}mathbf\{v\}(\textbackslash{}mathbf\{x\})\} -\/ \textbackslash{}int\+\_\+\{\textbackslash{}partial\textbackslash{}\+Omega\+\_\+N\} \textbackslash{}mathbf\{t\}(\textbackslash{}mathbf\{x\}) \textbackslash{}cdot \textbackslash{}mathbf\{v\}(\textbackslash{}mathbf\{x\}) = 0 \$\$

here, \$\textbackslash{}mathbf\{t\}(\textbackslash{}mathbf\{x\})\$ is the traction vector imposed on Neumann boundary \$\textbackslash{}\+Omega\+\_\+N\$, and \$\textbackslash{}varepsilon(\textbackslash{}mathbf\{u\}(\textbackslash{}mathbf\{x\})) = \textbackslash{}varepsilon\+\_\+\{ij\}(\textbackslash{}mathbf\{u\}(\textbackslash{}mathbf\{x\}))\$ is the strain tensor given by

\$\$ \textbackslash{}varepsilon\+\_\+\{ij\}(\textbackslash{}mathbf\{u\}) = \textbackslash{}frac\{1\}\{2\}(\textbackslash{}frac\{\textbackslash{}partial\{u\}\+\_\+i\}\{\textbackslash{}partial\{x\}\+\_\+j\} + \textbackslash{}frac\{\textbackslash{}partial\{u\}\+\_\+j\}\{\textbackslash{}partial\{x\}\+\_\+i\} ) \$\$\hypertarget{md__tmp_opencode_sharc_src_CAWF_Elasticity_Readme_autotoc_md25}{}\doxysection{An example elasticity solver}\label{md__tmp_opencode_sharc_src_CAWF_Elasticity_Readme_autotoc_md25}
We will provide a simple 2D bar problem example. The physics solved is a bar that bends under its own load. This XML configuration file is used for setting up an Elasticity problem simulation in Arcane\+FEM. Below is a detailed explanation of each section in the configuration for one of the tests {\ttfamily bar.\+2\+D.\+Dirichlet.\+body\+Force.\+arc}.

\doxysubparagraph*{Mesh Configuration}

The mesh configuration section specifies the mesh file to be used in the simulation\+:


\begin{DoxyCode}{0}
\DoxyCodeLine{<\textcolor{keywordtype}{meshes}>}
\DoxyCodeLine{  <\textcolor{keywordtype}{mesh}>}
\DoxyCodeLine{    <\textcolor{keywordtype}{filename}>\textcolor{keyword}{meshes}/\textcolor{keyword}{bar.msh}</\textcolor{keywordtype}{filename}>}
\DoxyCodeLine{      <\textcolor{keywordtype}{subdivider}>}
\DoxyCodeLine{        <\textcolor{keywordtype}{nb-\/subdivision}>0</\textcolor{keywordtype}{nb-\/subdivision}>}
\DoxyCodeLine{      </\textcolor{keywordtype}{subdivider}>}
\DoxyCodeLine{  </\textcolor{keywordtype}{mesh}>}
\DoxyCodeLine{</\textcolor{keywordtype}{meshes}>}

\end{DoxyCode}



\begin{DoxyItemize}
\item {\bfseries{Mesh File\+:}} Defines the mesh file {\ttfamily bar.\+msh} to be used in the simulation. Note that this file should be compatible with version 4.\+1 {\ttfamily .msh} format from {\ttfamily Gmsh}. The mesh here is a traingular mesh.
\item {\bfseries{Subdivision}}\+: {\ttfamily nb-\/subdivision} here allows to split the mesh to produce a finer mesh from a coarse mesh. It is practical to use for producing meshes for large-\/scale simulations.
\end{DoxyItemize}

\doxysubparagraph*{FEM Configuration}

The Finite Element Method (FEM) configuration is provided in the {\ttfamily bar.\+2\+D.\+Dirichlet.\+body\+Force.\+arc}.


\begin{DoxyCode}{0}
\DoxyCodeLine{<\textcolor{keywordtype}{fem}>}
\DoxyCodeLine{    <\textcolor{keywordtype}{E}>21.0\textcolor{keyword}{e5}</\textcolor{keywordtype}{E}>}
\DoxyCodeLine{    <\textcolor{keywordtype}{nu}>0.28</\textcolor{keywordtype}{nu}>}
\DoxyCodeLine{    <\textcolor{keywordtype}{f}>\textcolor{keyword}{NULL} -\/1.0</\textcolor{keywordtype}{f}>}
\DoxyCodeLine{    <\textcolor{keywordtype}{boundary-\/conditions}>}
\DoxyCodeLine{      <\textcolor{keywordtype}{dirichlet}>}
\DoxyCodeLine{        <\textcolor{keywordtype}{surface}>\textcolor{keyword}{left}</\textcolor{keywordtype}{surface}>}
\DoxyCodeLine{        <\textcolor{keywordtype}{value}>0.0 0.0</\textcolor{keywordtype}{value}>}
\DoxyCodeLine{      </\textcolor{keywordtype}{dirichlet}>}
\DoxyCodeLine{    </\textcolor{keywordtype}{boundary-\/conditions}>}
\DoxyCodeLine{</\textcolor{keywordtype}{fem}>}

\end{DoxyCode}


Let us explain this point wise


\begin{DoxyItemize}
\item {\bfseries{Material Properties\+:}} The Young\textquotesingle{}s Modulus (E) for the material, defined as {\ttfamily 21.\+0e5}. The Poisson\textquotesingle{}s Ratio (nu) for the material, defined as {\ttfamily 0.\+28}.
\end{DoxyItemize}


\begin{DoxyCode}{0}
\DoxyCodeLine{<\textcolor{keywordtype}{E}>21.0\textcolor{keyword}{e5}</\textcolor{keywordtype}{E}>}
\DoxyCodeLine{<\textcolor{keywordtype}{nu}>0.28</\textcolor{keywordtype}{nu}>}

\end{DoxyCode}

\begin{DoxyItemize}
\item {\bfseries{Source Term / Body Force\+:}} The source term or body force is present in in Y direction hence (f2) in the PDE, set to {\ttfamily -\/1.\+0}.
\end{DoxyItemize}


\begin{DoxyCode}{0}
\DoxyCodeLine{<\textcolor{keywordtype}{f}>\textcolor{keyword}{NULL} -\/1.0</\textcolor{keywordtype}{f}>}

\end{DoxyCode}

\begin{DoxyItemize}
\item {\bfseries{Dirichlet Method\+:}} The boundary condition on the specified surface ({\ttfamily left}) with given values for {\ttfamily u1} and {\ttfamily u2}, which we set to 0 since the end is clamped. Please note that it is expected the mesh {\ttfamily bar.\+msh} contains the physical group {\ttfamily left}.
\end{DoxyItemize}


\begin{DoxyCode}{0}
\DoxyCodeLine{<\textcolor{keywordtype}{boundary-\/conditions}>}
\DoxyCodeLine{  <\textcolor{keywordtype}{dirichlet}>}
\DoxyCodeLine{    <\textcolor{keywordtype}{surface}>\textcolor{keyword}{left}</\textcolor{keywordtype}{surface}>}
\DoxyCodeLine{    <\textcolor{keywordtype}{value}>0.0 0.0</\textcolor{keywordtype}{value}>}
\DoxyCodeLine{  </\textcolor{keywordtype}{dirichlet}>}
\DoxyCodeLine{</\textcolor{keywordtype}{boundary-\/conditions}>}

\end{DoxyCode}


\doxysubparagraph*{Post-\/\+Processing Configuration}

The post-\/processing configuration is specified to control how and when results are saved\+:


\begin{DoxyCode}{0}
\DoxyCodeLine{<\textcolor{keywordtype}{arcane-\/post-\/processing}>}
\DoxyCodeLine{  <\textcolor{keywordtype}{output-\/period}>1</\textcolor{keywordtype}{output-\/period}>}
\DoxyCodeLine{  <\textcolor{keywordtype}{format} \textcolor{keyword}{name}=\textcolor{stringliteral}{"{}VtkHdfV2PostProcessor"{}} />}
\DoxyCodeLine{  <\textcolor{keywordtype}{output}>}
\DoxyCodeLine{    <\textcolor{keywordtype}{variable}>\textcolor{keyword}{U}</\textcolor{keywordtype}{variable}>}
\DoxyCodeLine{  </\textcolor{keywordtype}{output}>}
\DoxyCodeLine{</\textcolor{keywordtype}{arcane-\/post-\/processing}>}

\end{DoxyCode}



\begin{DoxyItemize}
\item {\bfseries{Output Period\+:}} Sets the interval at which results are saved.
\item {\bfseries{Format\+:}} Specifies the format for the output files ({\ttfamily Vtk\+Hdf\+V2\+Post\+Processor}).
\item {\bfseries{Output Variables\+:}} Lists the variables ({\ttfamily U}) which is the displacement vector to be included in the output.
\end{DoxyItemize}\hypertarget{md__tmp_opencode_sharc_src_CAWF_Elasticity_Readme_autotoc_md26}{}\doxysubsubsection{Post Process}\label{md__tmp_opencode_sharc_src_CAWF_Elasticity_Readme_autotoc_md26}
For post processing the {\ttfamily Mesh0.\+hdf} file is outputted (in {\ttfamily output/depouillement/vtkhdfv2} folder), which can be read by PARAVIS. The output is of the \$\textbackslash{}mathbb\{P\}\+\_\+1\$ FE order (on nodes).
